agent1_snippets.title: Serotonin Syndrome -- Etiology
agent1_snippets.content: The 5-HT1 receptors are inhibitory G-protein-coupled receptors and have 5-subtypes: 5-HT1A, 5-HT1B, 5-HT1D, 5-HT1E, 5-HT1F. Several medications are prescribed to target these receptor subtypes. Buspirone targets 5-HT1A, and the antimigraine agents sumatriptan and zolmitriptan target 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D. [9] The 5-HT2 receptor family are excitatory G-protein-coupled receptors with 3 subtypes: 5-HT2A, 5-HT2B, and 5-HT2C. The 5-HT2A receptor is a common target of most second-generation antipsychotic medications that antagonize the receptor. Notably, 5-HT2A antagonism is suspected to shunt serotonin to available 5-HT1A receptors not blocked by antipsychotics, which may be a risk factor for precipitating serotonin syndrome. [12] 5-HT2C is targeted by the weight-loss drug lorcaserin (withdrawn from the United States market in 2020). [13] The 5-HT3 receptor is an excitatory ligand-gated ion channel most relevant for initiating emesis when activated. Ondansetron is a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist that directs antiemetic properties. The 5-HT4 receptor is a stimulatory G-coupled-protein receptor that increases gastric motility. Prucalopride is a 5-HT4 agonist that treats constipation. The 5-HT5 receptors are G-protein-coupled receptors comprised of 2 subtypes, 5-HT5A, and 5-HT5B, but have little known function and are unknown targets of medications. The 5-HT6 and 5-HT7 receptors are also stimulatory G-protein-coupled receptors, with some antidepressants and antipsychotics modulating activity through off-targeting, but no medications currently target 5-HT6 or 5-HT7. The etiology of serotonin syndrome results from cellular toxicity of excess target post-synaptic 5-HT stimulation. [9]

agent1_snippets.title: Antiemetics, Selective 5-HT3 Antagonists -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: The 5-HT3 receptor antagonists available on the US market include dolasetron, granisetron, palonosetron, and ondansetron. [1] Dolanestron, granisetron, and ondansetron are first-generation 5-HT3 receptor antagonists. Palonosetron is a second-generation 5-HT3 receptor antagonist. [2] Tropisetron and ramosetron are not available in the US. [1]

agent1_snippets.title: Constipation  -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: An additional option, prucalopride, a selective agonist of serotonin 5-HT 4 receptors, induces and increases the amplitude of colonic high-amplitude propagated contractions, thereby increasing bowel movement frequency and relieving constipation symptoms. The recommended dose is 2 mg once daily in adults and 1 mg daily in individuals with significant renal impairment. Side effects include headache, abdominal pain, nausea, and diarrhea. Prucalopride is contraindicated in patients with Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, intestinal perforation or obstruction, and toxic megacolon. [29] Patients with disorders such as colonic inertia may require total abdominal colectomy with ileorectal anastomosis.

agent1_snippets.title: Ondansetron -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: Ondansetron is a selective 5-HT3 serotonin-receptor antagonist used for its antiemetic properties. It is one of the four FDA-approved 5-HT3 serotonin-receptor antagonists used to combat nausea and vomiting, including granisetron, dolasetron, and the second-generation drug, palonosetron. [7]

agent1_snippets.title: Gastroparesis -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Treatment
agent1_snippets.content: Other off-label medications aimed at symptom relief include domperidone, centrally-acting antidepressants, and the serotonin type 4 (5-HT4) partial agonist tegaserod. [32] Domperidone is a dopamine-D2 antagonist that works similarly to metoclopramide to improve gastric emptying and decrease nausea and vomiting. The FDA restricts domperidone use in the United States; it is currently under review as an investigational drug. The initial dosage of domperidone is usually 10mg 3 times daily but can be increased to 20 mg per dose. Significant adverse effects of domperidone include cardiac arrhythmias and hyperprolactinemia; drug reactions are prevalent. Monitoring the QTc interval in patients taking domperidone is recommended; avoid or discontinue use if the QTc exceeds 450 ms.

agent1_snippets.title: Gastroparesis -- Treatment / Management -- Investigational Therapies
agent1_snippets.content: Multiple prospective treatments for gastroparesis are under investigation. Granisetron, a 5-HT3 antagonist administered as a transdermal patch, appears to improve gastroparesis-related nausea and vomiting. [36] Similarly, a randomized control trial of aprepitant, a neurokinin-1 receptor antagonist, showed symptom improvement in gastroparesis patients. [37] Cannabinoids, such as dronabinol, have been considered in the treatment of gastroparesis symptoms but have not been studied in clinical trials. However, cannabinoids can also delay gastric emptying. Acupuncture has proven effective for diabetic gastroparesis in randomized control trials. [31] Other investigational therapies undergoing evaluation include elamorelin, a pentapeptide ghrelin-receptor agonist; the 5HT-4 receptor agonists prucalopride and velusetrag; and metoclopramide nasal spray. [31] [38] [39]

agent1_snippets.title: Viloxazine -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: The exact mechanism of action of viloxazine remains uncertain; however, research suggests that it might function as a modulating agent of serotonin and norepinephrine. [1] In addition to inhibiting norepinephrine transport, viloxazine has demonstrated the ability to increase 5HT levels in the prefrontal cortex. Notably, the drug acts as an agonist at the 5HT2C receptor and an antagonist at the 5HT2B receptor. [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Metoclopramide -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: Metoclopramide works by antagonizing central and peripheral dopamine-two receptors (D2) in the medullary chemoreceptor trigger zone in the area postrema, usually stimulated by levodopa or apomorphine. It achieves this by decreasing the sensitivity of visceral afferent nerves that transmit from the gastrointestinal system to the vomiting center in the area postrema in the chemoreceptor trigger zone. [13] In addition to antagonizing dopamine receptors, metoclopramide is an antagonist at 5HT3 (type 3 serotonin receptors) and an agonist at 5HT4 receptors. [14] [15] Metoclopramide also blocks the antiperistaltic effects of apomorphine, allowing metoclopramide to slow apomorphine's inhibition of gastric emptying, thereby accelerating gastric emptying by increasing the amplitude and duration of esophageal contractions. Consequently, it increases the resting tone of the lower esophageal sphincter while simultaneously relaxing the duodenal bulb and pyloric sphincter, thereby increasing the peristalsis of the duodenum and jejunum. [16] [17]

agent1_snippets.content: An umbrella systematic review evaluating pharmacologic treatments for irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) assessed patient outcomes associated with 5-HT-3 antagonists, 5-HT4 agonists, guanylate cyclase-C agonists, antispasmodics, and alosetron. According to the review's findings, alosetron exhibited beneficial effects, including relief of global IBS symptoms. [35]

agent1_snippets.title: Lumateperone -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: The discovery of the role of 5HT-2A receptor antagonism in reducing the adverse motor effects caused by D2 receptor blockade has significantly improved treatment options available for those with schizophrenia. However, even with the repertoire of antipsychotics introduced in the past few decades, they have generally been more effective against positive symptoms rather than negative symptoms of schizophrenia. Additionally, they are associated with an increased incidence of adverse metabolic effects, such as weight gain and hyperprolactinemia. [2] As a result, an antipsychotic with decreased incidence of side effects and a broader range of efficacy against schizophrenia would provide a valuable addition to current treatments. [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Buspirone -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: Buspirone is classified in the azapirone drug class. It has a strong affinity for serotonin 5HT1a receptors, where it acts as a partial agonist, which some researchers believe produces the preponderance of clinical effects. It also has a weak affinity for serotonin 5HT2 receptors and acts as a weak antagonist of dopamine D2 autoreceptors. There is no effect on benzodiazepine GABA receptors. The underlying mechanism behind how the partial 5HT1a agonism translates into clinical results remains largely unknown. It is proposed from increased serotonergic activity in the amygdala and other parts of the brain's anxiety/fear circuitry. Due to the delayed anxiolytic effects seen clinically, buspirone likely provides relief through adaptations in 5HT1a receptors. [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Antiemetics, Selective 5-HT3 Antagonists -- Administration
agent1_snippets.content: 5-HT3 receptor antagonists come in various formulations, offering multiple administration routes. [20] These include oral tablets, orally disintegrating tablets, oral soluble film, oral solution, intramuscular (IM) injection, intravenous (IV) injection, subcutaneous (SubQ) injection, and transdermal patch. [21] [22] Palonosetron comes in an IV formulation. Ondansetron is available as oral tablets, orally disintegrating tablets, an oral solution, and IV and IM formulations. Dolasetron comes in an oral tablet and IV solution. Granisetron is available as an oral tablet, oral solution, transdermal patch, an IV formulation, and extended-release SubQ injection. The extended-release subcutaneous formulation of granisetron is long-acting and provides a therapeutic drug concentration for up to 5 days post-injection and is effective for delayed CINV, in addition to acute CINV. [10] Common dosing and administration regimens for adults include, but are not limited to:

agent2_snippets.content: Drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders constitute a therapeutic category that encompasses both serotonin receptor antagonists for functional gastrointestinal disorders and antispasmodics in combination with psycholeptics. Serotonin receptor antagonists for functional gastrointestinal disorders represent a subclass of these drugs and include cilansetron as a specific member. This organization of drug classes clarifies how various pharmacologic agents, such as cilansetron and combined antispasmodic‑psycholeptic formulations, are grouped under the broader umbrella of treatments for functional gastrointestinal conditions, aiding clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies for patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1311
agent2_snippets.content: Serotonin (5HT3) antagonists constitute a class of antiemetics and antinauseants that act by blocking 5‑HT3 receptors to prevent and treat nausea and vomiting, particularly in settings such as chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and postoperative recovery. Tropisetron is a specific drug that belongs to this class, exemplifying the therapeutic application of serotonin (5HT3) antagonism. By inhibiting the serotonergic pathways that trigger emesis, both the broader class of serotonin (5HT3) antagonists and individual agents like tropisetron play a crucial role in improving patient comfort and outcomes during treatments associated with high emetogenic risk.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 715
agent2_snippets.content: Serotonin (5HT3) antagonists constitute a class of antiemetics and antinauseants. Tropisetron is a member of this class, so it functions as an antiemetic and antinauseant through 5‑HT3 receptor blockade. In addition to its pharmacologic classification, tropisetron belongs to the broader chemical group “carboxylic acid and/or carboxylic acid derivative‑containing product,” a category that also includes phenyl salicylate. Both tropisetron and phenyl salicylate share the structural feature of containing a carboxylic acid or its derivative, linking them chemically, while tropisetron’s role as a serotonin (5HT3) antagonist defines its therapeutic use. Recognizing these overlapping chemical and therapeutic relationships helps clinicians understand the dual classification of agents like tropisetron and the broader family of carboxylic acid derivatives that encompass both therapeutic drugs and other compounds such as phenyl salicylate.

agent2_snippets.content: Alosetron and cilansetron are pharmacologic agents classified as serotonin receptor antagonists for functional gastrointestinal disorders. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2145
agent2_snippets.content: Bromodiphenhydramine and pimavanserin are pharmacologic agents that may be employed in the treatment of Parkinson’s disease. Bromodiphenhydramine, an antihistamine with anticholinergic activity, has potential utility for alleviating motor symptoms associated with the neurodegenerative disorder. Pimavanserin, a selective serotonin‑5‑HT₂A inverse agonist, is also recognized for its possible therapeutic role in Parkinson’s disease, particularly for managing psychotic features and may provide broader symptomatic benefit. Recognizing both drugs as possible treatment options expands therapeutic considerations for clinicians managing patients with Parkinson’s disease.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 336
agent2_snippets.content: Drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders comprise several therapeutic subgroups that are employed to manage symptoms of functional GI conditions. One important subgroup is serotonin receptor antagonists for functional gastrointestinal disorders, which includes agents such as alosetron that act by blocking serotonin receptors to reduce visceral hypersensitivity and improve bowel function. Another distinct subgroup consists of antispasmodics in combination with psycholeptics, a formulation that pairs muscle‑relaxing antispasmodic agents with central nervous system‑acting psycholeptic drugs to provide both peripheral and central relief of gastrointestinal spasm and discomfort. Together, these categories illustrate the diverse pharmacologic strategies—serotonin receptor blockade and combined antispasmodic‑psycholeptic therapy—used within the broader class of drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders to achieve symptom control and enhance patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2233
agent2_snippets.content: Ondansetron is a medication that belongs to the class of serotonin (5‑HT3) receptor antagonists, which block serotonin receptors to prevent nausea and vomiting. Antipsychotics constitute a separate pharmacologic category used to treat psychotic disorders, and acepromazine is an example of a drug that is classified as an antipsychotic (a phenothiazine tranquilizer). Although both ondansetron and acepromazine are drugs, they are members of distinct therapeutic classes with different mechanisms of action; ondansetron is not an antipsychotic, while acepromazine exemplifies the antipsychotic class. Recognizing these differences aids clinicians in selecting appropriate treatments and avoiding confusion between medication categories.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1987
agent2_snippets.content: Anxiolytics constitute a therapeutic class that includes several benzodiazepine agents—bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam—as well as the non‑benzodiazepine anxiolytic buspiron. All of these drugs share the primary function of reducing anxiety symptoms, though they differ in pharmacokinetic profiles, potency, and specific clinical indications. Benzodiazepines such as bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam act by enhancing the inhibitory effects of gamma‑aminobutyric acid (GABA) at the GABA‑A receptor, leading to rapid anxiolysis, sedation, muscle relaxation, and anticonvulsant effects. Buspiron, in contrast, exerts its anxiolytic action primarily through partial agonism at serotonin 5‑HT1A receptors, offering anxiety relief with a lower risk of sedation and dependence. Recognizing that these diverse agents all belong to the anxiolytic category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on the patient’s specific needs, risk of side effects, and potential for dependence.

agent2_snippets.content: Metoclopramide is classified as a member of the propulsives group, indicating its role as a gastrointestinal motility‑enhancing agent. In addition, metoclopramide belongs to the dopamine antagonist class, reflecting its pharmacologic action of blocking dopamine receptors. Promazine is also identified as a dopamine antagonist, sharing this mechanism of action with metoclopramide. Together, these relationships highlight that both metoclopramide and promazine act as dopamine antagonists, while metoclopramide uniquely combines this activity with its membership in the propulsives category.

agent2_snippets.content: Antipsychotics are a class of psychiatric medications used to manage psychotic disorders such as schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, while ondansetron is a selective 5‑HT3 receptor antagonist that is employed primarily as an antiemetic to prevent nausea and vomiting associated with chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and surgery. These two agents belong to distinct therapeutic categories; antipsychotics act on dopamine and other neurotransmitter pathways to alleviate psychosis, whereas ondansetron blocks serotonin receptors in the gastrointestinal tract and central nervous system to control emesis. Consequently, antipsychotics are not a type of ondansetron, and each drug class serves different clinical purposes in patient care.
